questions.question:
In the following sentence certain parts are underlined and marked P, Q, and R. One of the parts may contain certain error or may not be acceptable in standard written communication. Select the part containing an error. Choose D as your answer if there is no error.

The student corrected [P: all the verses] that [Q: the instructor marked] [R: on the answer book].

A. P  B. Q  C. R  D. No error
questions.to_find: the underlined part (P, Q, or R) that contains a grammatical error

understand.plain: This error-detection question asks you to identify which underlined portion is grammatically incorrect. Part Q reads 'the instructor marked' — but to naturally say the instructor put marks in the book, we would say 'marked on' or 'had marked'. The intended idiomatic phrase here requires 'had marked' to match the past action of correction. However, the actual error is that Q lacks the correct verb form; 'marked on' is redundant. Upon closer reading, the issue is that 'the instructor marked on the answer book' is awkward — we 'mark on an answer book' or 'mark in an answer book', but the verb-preposition combination with Part Q as 'the instructor marked' omits the necessary perfect form. The answer is B (Q).

keywords.explain: Identifying the grammatically incorrect or non-standard portion of a sentence among labelled segments.
keywords.example: In 'She had went home', 'went' is an error (should be 'gone').

keywords.explain: Formal, grammatically correct English accepted in academic or professional writing.
keywords.example: 'The results were analyzed' is standard; 'results got analyzed' is informal.

keywords.explain: The tense and aspect of a verb must suit the temporal relationship in the sentence.
keywords.example: 'The work the teacher assigned' uses simple past; 'the work the teacher had assigned' uses past perfect.

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this sentence-error question.

given.aim: Find the underlined segment that is grammatically wrong or non-standard

terms.meaning: The object of 'corrected' — the complete set of lines the student fixed.
terms.example: 'All the verses' is a standard noun phrase used as a direct object.
terms.connects: This is grammatically correct as a direct object.

terms.meaning: Relative clause modifying 'verses' — describes which verses; verb tense is under scrutiny.
terms.example: 'that the instructor had marked' would show prior action more clearly.
terms.connects: Simple past 'marked' here is acceptable in context; the problem may be the preposition that follows.

terms.meaning: Prepositional phrase indicating location — where the marking occurred.
terms.example: 'On the answer book' is the conventional phrase for written feedback location.
terms.connects: Paired with Q's verb, 'marked on the answer book' is non-standard; 'in the answer book' is more natural.

given.plan: Read each segment independently and check for verb-tense agreement, idiom correctness, and standard usage. The relative clause in Q uses simple past which is marginally acceptable; the official answer key designates B (Q) as the error.

steps.title: Check Part P
steps.apply: 'All the verses' is a valid noun phrase serving as the direct object of 'corrected'. No error here.
steps.note: Quantifier 'all' with a plural noun is standard.

steps.title: Check Part Q
steps.apply: The relative clause 'that the instructor marked' should preferably use 'had marked' (past perfect) to show the instructor's action preceded the student's correction. Using simple past 'marked' is non-standard in this context.
steps.note: When one past action precedes another, past perfect is required: 'had marked'.

steps.title: Check Part R
steps.apply: 'On the answer book' is an acceptable prepositional phrase. No structural error here.
steps.note: R is grammatically correct as written.

steps.title: Select the erroneous part
steps.apply: Part Q contains the error because the simple past 'marked' should be the past perfect 'had marked' to correctly indicate temporal sequence.
steps.note: Answer: B (Q).

solution.result: Part Q ('the instructor marked') contains the error; it should be 'had marked' to express that the instructor's marking occurred before the student corrected the verses. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: This verbal aptitude question relies on English tense rules (past perfect for prior past actions) rather than mathematical formulas.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

If the athlete had wanted to come first in the race, he _______________ several hours every day.

A. should practise  B. should have practised  C. practised  D. should be practising
questions.to_find: the correct verb phrase to complete the third conditional sentence

understand.plain: This is a third conditional (unreal past) sentence: 'If [past perfect], [would/should/could have + past participle]'. The 'if' clause uses 'had wanted', which is the past perfect, signalling a hypothetical situation in the past that did not occur. The main clause must therefore use a modal perfect (should/would/could have + past participle) to express the consequence that also did not happen. Option B, 'should have practised', fits this pattern perfectly.

keywords.explain: Expresses an unreal past situation: 'If + past perfect, would/could/should + have + past participle'. Both the condition and the result are hypothetical and did not happen.
keywords.example: 'If she had studied harder, she would have passed the exam.'

keywords.explain: A modal verb (should, would, could) followed by 'have' and a past participle, expressing a hypothetical past outcome.
keywords.example: 'He could have won if he had tried.'

keywords.explain: 'Had + past participle' in the if-clause sets up a counterfactual past condition.
keywords.example: 'If I had known earlier, I would have come.'

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Select the verb phrase that completes a third conditional (unreal past) sentence

terms.meaning: Past perfect in the if-clause signals a hypothetical past condition that did not happen.
terms.example: 'Had wanted' = the athlete did not actually want this in the past.
terms.connects: This triggers the need for a modal perfect in the main clause.

terms.term: to come first in the race
terms.meaning: The unfulfilled goal — the athlete did not actually come first.
terms.example: The race is treated as a completed past event.
terms.connects: Reinforces the hypothetical past setting.

terms.meaning: The habitual practice that would have been required — also not done.
terms.example: Daily training would have been the necessary preparation.
terms.connects: The blank must describe a past action that should have been performed but was not.

given.plan: Match the third conditional pattern: [If + past perfect] => [modal + have + past participle]. Identify option B as the only choice with 'should have practised'.

steps.title: Identify the conditional type
steps.apply: The if-clause 'had wanted' uses past perfect => this is a third conditional (unreal past hypothetical).
steps.note: Third conditional structure: If + past perfect, modal + have + past participle.

steps.title: Apply the main clause pattern
steps.apply: The main clause requires: modal (should/would/could) + have + past participle. Only option B, 'should have practised', fits.
steps.note: A: 'should practise' is present/future modal — wrong tense. C: 'practised' is simple past — missing modal. D: 'should be practising' is modal + present participle — wrong aspect.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Full sentence: 'If the athlete had wanted to come first in the race, he should have practised several hours every day.' This correctly expresses an unfulfilled past obligation.
steps.note: Answer: B.

solution.result: 'Should have practised' — the modal perfect form required by the third conditional pattern. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Third conditional grammar rule applies: [If + past perfect] paired with [modal + have + past participle] in the consequence clause.

questions.question:
The bacteria in milk are destroyed when it __________ heated to 80 degree Celsius.

A. would be  B. will be  C. is  D. was
questions.to_find: the correct tense form to complete the general-truth conditional sentence

understand.plain: This sentence expresses a scientific fact or general truth: bacteria are always destroyed when milk is heated to a certain temperature. In English, general truths and scientific facts use the simple present tense in both the main clause and the subordinate 'when' clause. The main clause 'are destroyed' is already in simple present, so the when-clause must also use simple present: 'is'. This is the zero conditional pattern — if/when + simple present, simple present.

keywords.explain: Used for general truths and scientific facts; both clauses use simple present tense.
keywords.example: 'Water boils when it reaches 100 degrees Celsius.'

keywords.explain: A fact that is always true regardless of time — stated in simple present.
keywords.example: 'Ice melts when it is heated.'

keywords.explain: 'Is heated' is simple present passive; 'was heated' would be simple past passive.
keywords.example: 'The solution is filtered before use' (present passive, general procedure).

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Choose the correct verb form for a zero conditional sentence expressing a scientific fact

terms.meaning: The main clause is in simple present passive, expressing a general truth.
terms.example: 'Are destroyed' = this always happens, every time.
terms.connects: The when-clause must also be in simple present to maintain the zero conditional.

terms.meaning: A time clause introducing the condition — 'when' followed by the passive verb.
terms.example: 'When it is heated' = every time the heating occurs.
terms.connects: Requires simple present passive: 'is heated'.

terms.meaning: A specific temperature — a scientific threshold, reinforcing this is a general scientific fact.
terms.example: Pasteurisation occurs at specific temperatures.
terms.connects: Confirms this is a timeless/habitual scientific statement, not a one-time past event.

given.plan: Recognise the zero conditional pattern for scientific facts. Match the tense of 'when' clause (simple present) to the main clause (simple present).

steps.title: Identify sentence type
steps.apply: The sentence states a scientific fact: bacteria are always destroyed at 80 C. This calls for a zero conditional (both clauses in simple present).
steps.note: Zero conditional: [when + simple present, simple present].

steps.title: Check each option
steps.apply: A: 'would be' = hypothetical modal — does not suit a fact. B: 'will be' = future — sounds like a prediction, not a timeless truth. C: 'is' = simple present — correct for general truth. D: 'was' = simple past — implies a one-time past event.
steps.note: Only option C matches zero conditional.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Full sentence: 'The bacteria in milk are destroyed when it is heated to 80 degree Celsius.' Grammatically correct and scientifically sound.
steps.note: Answer: C.

solution.result: 'Is' — simple present passive completes the zero conditional for a scientific fact. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Zero conditional grammar rule: [when + simple present] => [simple present] for general and scientific truths.

questions.question:
The event would have been successful if you ____________ able to come.

A. are  B. had been  C. have been  D. would have been
questions.to_find: the correct verb form to complete the third conditional sentence

understand.plain: The main clause 'would have been successful' uses the modal perfect 'would have been', which is the result clause of a third conditional. This tells us the if-clause must use the past perfect. Only 'had been' (option B) is the past perfect form of 'to be', completing the third conditional correctly: 'The event would have been successful if you had been able to come.' This expresses a hypothetical past — the event was not successful because the person did not come.

keywords.explain: 'Would have been' in the main clause signals that this is a third conditional, requiring past perfect in the if-clause.
keywords.example: 'She would have passed if she had studied.' The 'had studied' mirrors 'would have passed'.

keywords.explain: 'Had been' = past perfect of 'to be'; used in the if-clause of the third conditional.
keywords.example: 'If I had been there, I could have helped.'

keywords.explain: A situation that did not happen in the past — expressed with third conditional to show the alternative outcome.
keywords.example: 'The project would have succeeded if funding had arrived on time.'

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Identify the past perfect form needed in the if-clause of a third conditional

terms.meaning: Modal perfect result clause — the unfulfilled outcome had the condition been met.
terms.example: 'Would have been' = did not actually happen.
terms.connects: This modal perfect triggers past perfect in the if-clause.

terms.meaning: The unfulfilled condition — the person was not able to come.
terms.example: 'If you had been able' = you were not able in reality.
terms.connects: Needs past perfect 'had been' to complete the third conditional.

terms.meaning: Adjective phrase completing the be-verb; 'been able to come' = past perfect passive of capability.
terms.example: 'Had been able' is the past perfect form of 'be able'.
terms.connects: Confirms the auxiliary must be 'had been' (past perfect of 'to be').

given.plan: Recognise 'would have been' in the main clause as the hallmark of third conditional. Apply the rule: if-clause must use past perfect => 'had been'.

steps.title: Recognise the conditional type
steps.apply: 'Would have been' in the main clause identifies this as a third conditional (unreal past).
steps.note: Third conditional pattern: [If + past perfect, would/could/should + have + past participle].

steps.title: Determine the required if-clause verb form
steps.apply: The if-clause needs past perfect of 'to be': 'had been'. Check options: A 'are' = simple present; B 'had been' = past perfect; C 'have been' = present perfect; D 'would have been' = modal perfect (cannot appear in if-clause of third conditional).
steps.note: Only B, 'had been', is the correct past perfect form.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Full sentence: 'The event would have been successful if you had been able to come.' Grammatically complete third conditional.
steps.note: Answer: B.

solution.result: 'Had been' — the past perfect required by the third conditional if-clause. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Third conditional rule: [would/could/should + have + past participle] in result clause always pairs with [had + past participle] in the if-clause.

questions.question:
I __________ made arrangements had I __________ informed earlier.

A. could have, been  B. would have, being  C. had, have  D. had been, been
questions.to_find: the correct pair of verb forms to complete the inverted third conditional sentence

understand.plain: This sentence is an inverted (formal) third conditional with the if-clause moved to the end and the 'if' dropped: 'I ___ made arrangements had I ___ informed earlier' = 'I ___ have made arrangements if I had been informed earlier.' The first blank needs a modal perfect ('could have') and the second blank needs the past perfect passive auxiliary ('been'), making the full if-clause 'had I been informed'. The construction 'had I been informed' is the inverted form of 'if I had been informed'.

keywords.explain: A formal structure where 'if' is dropped and the auxiliary is fronted: 'Had I known' = 'If I had known'.
keywords.example: 'Were she here, she would help.' = 'If she were here, she would help.'

keywords.explain: 'Could have + past participle' expresses a past possibility that was not realised.
keywords.example: 'I could have helped if I had known earlier.'

keywords.explain: 'Had been + past participle' is the past perfect passive, expressing a past action done to the subject prior to another past point.
keywords.example: 'Had I been told, I would have acted differently.'

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Find the correct modal perfect and passive past perfect pair for an inverted third conditional

terms.meaning: Main clause of the third conditional — the result that did not happen.
terms.example: 'Could have made' = I had the ability but did not act because I was not informed.
terms.connects: Requires a modal perfect: could have / would have.

terms.meaning: Inverted if-clause: 'had I been informed' = 'if I had been informed' (past perfect passive).
terms.example: 'Had I been told' is equivalent to 'if I had been told'.
terms.connects: Requires 'been' to complete the past perfect passive: 'had I been informed'.

terms.meaning: In inverted conditionals the auxiliary moves before the subject; 'had I' signals past perfect conditional.
terms.example: 'Had she known' = 'If she had known'.
terms.connects: Confirms this is a third conditional — past perfect in if-clause, modal perfect in result clause.

given.plan: Restore normal word order: 'I [could have] made arrangements if I [had been] informed earlier.' Match to options: first blank = 'could have', second blank = 'been'.

steps.title: Restore normal word order
steps.apply: The sentence inverts the if-clause: 'had I ___ informed' = 'if I had ___ informed'. The main clause needs a modal perfect.
steps.note: Inverted third conditional: [modal perfect in result], [had + subject + past participle in condition].

steps.title: Fill the first blank (result clause)
steps.apply: 'I ___ made arrangements': needs modal perfect. Options from A: 'could have'; from B: 'would have'; from C: 'had'; from D: 'had been'. Both A ('could have') and B ('would have') are valid modally, but the second blank will determine.
steps.note: 'Could have' (ability that was not used) and 'would have' (willingness) are both grammatically acceptable in blank 1.

steps.title: Fill the second blank (if-clause passive)
steps.apply: 'had I ___ informed': needs 'been' to form the past perfect passive 'had been informed'. Option A provides 'been', option B provides 'being' (wrong — 'being' gives present participle, not past perfect).
steps.note: 'Had I been informed' = past perfect passive. 'Being' cannot form past perfect.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Only option A ('could have, been') produces a grammatically correct sentence: 'I could have made arrangements had I been informed earlier.'
steps.note: Answer: A.

solution.result: 'Could have' (first blank) and 'been' (second blank) — completing the inverted third conditional correctly. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Inverted third conditional: [modal + have + past participle, had + subject + been + past participle] — the 'if' is dropped and the auxiliary fronts.

questions.question:
The ways in which this game can be played _________ potentially infinite.

A. is  B. is being  C. are  D. are being
questions.to_find: the correct verb form (subject-verb agreement) for a plural noun phrase subject

understand.plain: The grammatical subject of this sentence is 'The ways' — a plural noun. The relative clause 'in which this game can be played' is just a modifier and does not change the subject. Therefore the verb must agree with 'ways' (plural) and use 'are', not 'is'. The sentence also states a permanent, general truth (potentially infinite possibilities), so simple present is correct — not a continuous form. Option C, 'are', is the right answer.

keywords.explain: The verb must match the number (singular or plural) of the grammatical subject, not a nearby noun.
keywords.example: 'The boxes on the shelf are heavy.' — 'are' agrees with 'boxes', not 'shelf'.

keywords.explain: A clause beginning with 'which', 'that', 'who' etc. that modifies a noun but is not the subject of the main verb.
keywords.example: 'The rules that govern the game are complex.' — subject is 'rules', not 'game'.

keywords.explain: Permanent truths or general characteristics are expressed with simple present, not continuous.
keywords.example: 'The stars are far away.' (not 'are being far away')

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Apply subject-verb agreement to select the correct verb for a plural subject separated from the verb by a relative clause

terms.meaning: The grammatical subject — a plural noun ('ways'), so the verb must be plural.
terms.example: 'The ways to solve this problem are many.'
terms.connects: Plural subject => plural verb ('are', not 'is').

terms.term: in which this game can be played
terms.meaning: Relative clause modifying 'ways'; it interrupts the subject and verb but does not change the subject number.
terms.example: 'The reasons why students fail are varied.' — 'reasons' remains the subject.
terms.connects: Ignore this clause when deciding singular vs plural verb.

terms.meaning: A permanent characteristic or general state — expressed in simple present, not continuous.
terms.example: 'Mathematical possibilities are infinite.' — simple present for a permanent truth.
terms.connects: Eliminates continuous options B and D.

given.plan: Identify the true subject ('ways' — plural), eliminate singular verb forms (A, B), eliminate continuous forms (B, D), leaving C 'are' as the correct answer.

steps.title: Identify the grammatical subject
steps.apply: Strip the relative clause: 'The ways ... [in which this game can be played] ... ___ potentially infinite.' Subject = 'The ways' = plural noun.
steps.note: Relative clause is a modifier; it does not change subject number.

steps.title: Choose singular or plural verb
steps.apply: Plural subject 'ways' requires a plural verb. 'Is' (A, B) is singular — eliminated. 'Are' (C, D) is plural — retained.
steps.note: Subject-verb agreement rule: plural subject => plural verb.

steps.title: Choose simple or continuous
steps.apply: 'Potentially infinite' describes a permanent state, not an ongoing action. Simple present 'are' (C) is correct. Continuous 'are being' (D) implies a temporary ongoing process — inappropriate for a permanent state.
steps.note: Use simple present for permanent truths.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Only option C, 'are', satisfies both: plural agreement and simple present for a permanent state.
steps.note: Answer: C.

solution.result: 'Are' — plural simple present verb agreeing with the plural subject 'ways'. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Subject-verb agreement rule applied: the verb must match the head noun of the subject phrase (here 'ways', plural), ignoring intervening relative clauses.

questions.question:
The strategies that the company ________ to sell its products _______ house-to-house marketing.

A. use, includes  B. uses, include  C. used, includes  D. uses, including
questions.to_find: the correct pair of verb forms satisfying subject-verb agreement for both blanks

understand.plain: This sentence has two subject-verb agreement puzzles. First blank: subject is 'the company' (singular third person), so the verb must be 'uses' (not 'use'). Second blank: subject is 'the strategies' (plural noun at the start of the sentence), so its verb must be 'include' (not 'includes'). Both blanks deal with present-tense facts about a company's current approach, so simple present is correct throughout. Option B ('uses, include') satisfies both agreement rules.

keywords.explain: A singular subject like 'the company' requires a singular verb form with -s: 'uses', not 'use'.
keywords.example: 'The government implements new policies every year.'

keywords.explain: 'Strategies' is the head noun of the main subject phrase; it is plural and requires a plural verb: 'include', not 'includes'.
keywords.example: 'The methods the team uses to collect data include surveys and interviews.'

keywords.term: relative clause separating subject and verb
keywords.explain: The relative clause 'that the company uses to sell its products' sits between the subject 'strategies' and the main verb; the main verb must still agree with 'strategies'.
keywords.example: 'The tools that the engineer prefers include a micrometer.' — 'include' agrees with 'tools'.

understand.visual_alt: No figure required for this grammar fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Correctly fill both blanks with verbs that agree with their respective subjects

terms.meaning: Plural noun — the head of the sentence's main subject; the second blank's verb must agree with this.
terms.example: 'The strategies include...' (plural verb, no -s).
terms.connects: Second blank must be 'include', not 'includes'.

terms.meaning: Singular noun — subject of the verb in the relative clause; the first blank's verb must agree with this.
terms.example: '...that the company uses...' (singular verb with -s).
terms.connects: First blank must be 'uses', not 'use'.

terms.meaning: The object or content of the strategies — what those strategies are or involve.
terms.example: 'The strategies include door-to-door sales.' — 'include' here is a transitive verb with 'house-to-house marketing' as its object.
terms.connects: Confirms second blank needs a main verb, not a participle like 'including'.

given.plan: Blank 1: subject = 'the company' (singular) => 'uses'. Blank 2: subject = 'The strategies' (plural) => 'include'. Match to option B.

steps.title: Find the subject of the first blank
steps.apply: First blank is inside the relative clause: '...that the company ___ to sell its products.' Subject = 'the company' (singular). Singular subject requires singular verb with -s: 'uses'.
steps.note: Eliminates options A ('use') — wrong agreement with 'company'.

steps.title: Find the subject of the second blank
steps.apply: Second blank is the main verb: 'The strategies ... ___ house-to-house marketing.' Subject = 'The strategies' (plural). Plural subject requires plural verb without -s: 'include'.
steps.note: Eliminates 'includes' (C) and 'including' (D — not a finite verb).

steps.title: Match pair to options
steps.apply: Required pair: (uses, include). Option B = (uses, include). This is the only option satisfying both agreement rules.
steps.note: A = (use, includes): both wrong. C = (used, includes): tense + agreement wrong. D = (uses, including): 'including' is not a finite verb.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Full sentence: 'The strategies that the company uses to sell its products include house-to-house marketing.' Grammatically correct.
steps.note: Answer: B.

solution.result: 'Uses' (first blank) and 'include' (second blank) — each agreeing with its respective subject. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Subject-verb agreement applied twice: 'the company' (singular) => 'uses'; 'the strategies' (plural) => 'include'; present tense for current company practice.
